title: The Morning Routine
__comment_scenes: === BACKGROUND SCENE ASSETS (Paths relative to main game directory) ===
__comment_global_hotspots: === GLOBAL HITBOX POSITIONING (Coordinates must be strings representing viewport percentage integers 0-100) ===
__comment_win_states: === END OF LEVEL UI CARD STRINGS ===
completeDialogue: You found it! Fantastic work, Bill. You already know how to use strings, functions, and dot notation! You are going to be a natural JavaScript Developer. Come on, let's head over to the Bakery to catch a thief!

phases.__comment_phase_config: scene must map to a key in the top-level scenes block. answer is the exact string match expected in #term-in.
phases.__comment_ui_panels: lesson is plain text rendered in the lesson tab panel. notes must be an array of strings parsed as list elements.
phases.lesson: console.log("...") is the most fundamental JavaScript command. It prints text to the terminal.
notes: Use console.log("text"); to talk to the computer.
phases.dialogue: Welcome to the agency, Rookie! I'm Chief Inspector Bunny. Before we can solve any mysteries, we need to make sure your Detective Terminal is online. In JavaScript, we make the computer speak using a command called console.log. Let's test it by saying hello!
phases.__comment_phase_hotspots: id matches top global_hotspots. Use \n to represent linebreaks inside raw monospace code strings.

active_hotspots.hover_text: Your detective terminal interface. It's uncalibrated.

phases.output:
Room scanned.
→ 3 interactive zones detected:
   • desk
   • plant
   • drawers
phases.lesson: A function is a named tool. scan("office") passes a string argument to tell the function what to scan.
notes: HINT: Use inspect("target"); to look closer.
phases.dialogue: Perfect, we are online! Now, I have a bit of a problem… I lost my main investigation journal somewhere in this messy office. Let's use the terminal to run a sweep of the room. To use a tool, you type its name, followed by your target inside quotes.

active_hotspots.hover_text: Ready for an environmental room scan sweep.

phases.output:
Inspecting desk...
→ Desk contains:
   • coffee_mug
   • open_book
   • clipboard
phases.lesson: Same function, different argument. The string you pass changes what the function targets.
notes: Objects have actions — connect with a dot: object.action();
phases.dialogue: Great job! The scanner found my desk, a potted plant, and some wooden drawers. I usually leave my journal right on my workspace. Let's use the inspect tool to look closer at it.

phases.output:
Reading open_book...
→ Webster's English Dictionary.
→ Nothing useful here.
phases.lesson: Pattern: object.method("argument"); Code can run successfully and still not solve the puzzle — that's exploration!
phases.dialogue: Hmm, no journal. But look! There is an open_book and a clipboard. In code, things are called 'objects', and we can make them do actions by connecting them with a dot. Let's try reading that book first to see if it's my journal.

active_hotspots.hover_text: An opened dictionary is propped up against the lamp.

phases.output:
Reading clipboard...
→ Note: 'Keep journal locked in drawers.
         Hide key in foliage.'
notes: CLUE: Journal is locked in the drawers.
CLUE: Key is hidden in the foliage (plant).
phases.dialogue: Ah, false alarm. That's just my dictionary. Nothing useful there! But that's okay, detectives have to explore! Let's check the clipboard instead. Remember the dot notation!

active_hotspots.hover_text: An agency clipboard holding maintenance logs.

phases.output:
Scanning plant...
→ Glint of metal detected!
→ A silver key is visible among the leaves.
phases.lesson: You used a function you already know on a new target based on a discovered clue.
notes: VISUAL: Silver key spotted in the plant leaves!
phases.dialogue: Aha! The note says I hid the spare key in the foliage… that must mean the potted plant! Use your inspect tool again, but this time change your target to the plant.

active_hotspots.hover_text: A decorative potted plant sitting in the light.

phases.output:
[Item Acquired: Silver Key] 🗝
→ Key added to inventory.
phases.lesson: self refers to you — the player object. self.take("key") invokes your pick up sequence.
notes: NOTE: You have the key. Now target the drawers.
phases.dialogue: There it is! A silver key! In this game, you are an object too, and your code name is self. To pick things up and put them in your inventory, you have to tell yourself to take them.

active_hotspots.hover_text: The key is glittering within the branches.

phases.output:
ERROR: The drawers are locked. Access denied.
→ ScopeError: container.isLocked === true
→ You must unlock the drawers before opening them!
phases.lesson: Code runs in exact order. An ERROR is just the system telling you what it needs next.
phases.dialogue: Got it! Now, the note said my journal is in the wooden drawers. Let's head over there and try to open them! Some commands don't need any targets inside the parentheses.

phases.output:
Click.
→ Security lock disengaged. 🔓
→ Padlock status: OPEN.
phases.lesson: You passed an item name string into a method as an execution argument.
notes: Lock disabled. Now safe to open.
phases.dialogue: Oops! Did you see that red Error? Computers follow exact logic—we can't open a locked door without unlocking it first! We need to use the unlock action and pass the key we found as our target.

active_hotspots.hover_text: The locking mechanism requires key authentication.

phases.output:
Drawers opening...
→ [Chief Inspector Bunny's Journal Acquired] 📓

★  LEVEL COMPLETE — The Morning Routine  ★
phases.lesson: Some methods require no arguments — just empty execution blocks: drawers.open().
phases.dialogue: Hear that click? The security lock is disabled! Now it is safe to open them. Try that exact same command from earlier, with the empty parentheses.

active_hotspots.hover_text: Unlocked State. Safe to pull open.
